exemplar.title: To what extent do external factors influence the accuracy and reliability of eyewitness memory in legal proceedings?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent do external factors influence the accuracy and reliability of eyewitness memory in legal proceedings?

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Topic is communicated accurately and effectively with clear and appropriate purpose and focus.
Research question is precisely stated, focused and tightly connected to the ensuing discussion.
weaknesses: Methodology description lacks explicit detail on source selection criteria, search strategy and screening procedures.
Operational definition of âexternal factorsâ is not provided, leading to occasional ambiguity in scope.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates a very strong command of Focus and Method overall. The topic and research question are communicated clearly and consistently, earning high marks in those strands. However, the literature-review methodology is not fully systematicâkey elements such as inclusion/exclusion criteria and search protocols are missing. On balance, this merits a band-3 descriptor (3â4 marks), and 4 out of 6 appropriately reflects the high quality tempered by that methodological gap.

strengths: Accurate and consistent use of subject-specific terminology (e.g., weapon focus effect, flashbulb memory, System 1 thinking).
Integration of theoretical concepts (dual-process model, cortisolâhippocampus mechanism) demonstrates coherent understanding.
weaknesses: Procedural details (sample sizes, effect sizes, statistical values) are inconsistently reported, limiting full assessment of generalisability.
Transitions between different perspectives (biological, cognitive, sociocultural) sometimes feel abrupt, reflecting uneven source integration.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Knowledge and Understanding is solid: terminology is precise and source application is generally coherent, justifying a band-3/4 level. The omission of key procedural specifics prevents placement in the top band. A score of 4 out of 6 reflects good subject knowledge with some lapses in consistent, detailed application of experimental data.

strengths: Analysis unpacks mechanisms such as cortisolâs hippocampal effects and schema-driven reconstruction with clarity.
Discussion weighs reliability versus unreliability arguments and integrates ethical/legal implications in a reasoned argument.
Research coverage is wide and mostly maintained relevance to the research question, distinguishing lab, field and naturalistic designs.
weaknesses: Depth of analysis is unevenâkey effect sizes and confidence intervals are rarely reported.
Recommendations for future research remain too broad; some conclusions overstate the evidence (e.g., equating eyewitness reliability with DNA).
Cultural bias critiques would be strengthened by referencing specific dimensions (e.g., individualism vs. collectivism) and recent replications.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Critical Thinking performance aligns with the lower half of the 7â9 band: the student offers clear, supported analysis and a partially critical evaluation, but statistical rigor and precision are inconsistent. A mid-band score of 5 out of 12 (scaled to reflect the overall mark constraint) appropriately rewards the strong analytical framework while recognising the identified shortcomings.

strengths: Structure fully conforms to IB conventions: title page, table of contents, numbered headings and logical progression.
Sections are clearly signposted, making navigation straightforward.
weaknesses: Reference list formatting is inconsistent (hanging indents, DOI and URL breaks).
Minor layout issues (footer misalignments, occasional irregular spacing) detract from overall professional finish.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation is generally effective, with all structural conventions met, but noticeable formatting inconsistencies limit the performance to the middle band. A score of 2 out of 4 reflects a clearly appropriate structure with some layout and formatting errors that prevent full marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Key psychological termsâweapon focus, flashbulb memory, schemas, System 1 thinkingâare employed accurately and consistently throughout.  Definitions are usually supplied on first use and then applied correctly in later analysis.  Occasional lay phrasing (e.g., âbad guysâ) does not undermine overall precision.  The essay therefore meets the standard for accurate and consistent use of terminology.

Basic layout featuresâdouble spacing, readable font, page numbersâare correctly applied, and in-text citations appear in APA style.  However, the reference list shows inconsistent hanging indents, DOI formatting varies, and a few URLs break mid-line.  Minor footer misalignments and occasional double spacing within paragraphs detract from professional finish, so the work fits the âsome considerations missing or incorrectâ band.

The student demonstrates solid command of core findings (e.g., Loftus & Palmer, weapon focus, dual-process model) and applies them appropriately to the argument.  Over thirty citations cover biological, cognitive and sociocultural perspectives, showing good breadth.  Nevertheless, procedural specifics such as sample size, effect sizes and statistical values are inconsistently reported, and transitions between biological and psychodynamic explanations sometimes feel abrupt, indicating good rather than excellent source integration.

The methodology is essentially a systematic literature review.  A healthy range of classic experiments, meta-analyses and naturalistic studies is selected; however, the essay does not explain *how* the sources were located, screened or prioritised, nor does it justify inclusion/exclusion criteria.  Participant demographics or statistical thresholds are only sporadically reported.  These omissions mean source selection appears informed but not fully systematic, fitting the âmostly completeâ rather than âcompleteâ descriptor.

The student introduces the topic clearly in the title, abstract-style opening and introduction, immediately establishing the legal-psychological context and clarifying why eyewitness reliability matters. Purpose and focus remain consistent from the introduction through the conclusion, with section headings that mirror the stated aim. Occasional ambiguity around the term âexternal factorsâ does not seriously impede understanding, so overall communication of the topic and its scope is both accurate and effective.

Research coverage is wide, spanning classic studies to 2018 meta-analyses, and each source is explicitly linked to the RQ.  The student differentiates between lab, field and naturalistic designs, commenting on ecological validity and sampling bias.  A few references (e.g., Freud 1899) are tangential, and cultural breadth is still predominantly US-centric, preventing a âconsistently excellentâ judgement, but overall relevance is strong and sustained.

The research question is explicitly stated in italics on the first page and is narrow enough to guide the ensuing literature review.  Each major subsection (weapon focus, stress, stereotypes, post-event information, flashbulb memories, confidence) maps directly onto the phrase âexternal factors.â  Cross-referencing of the RQ in the conclusion shows clear thread of focus.  Minor broad phrasing such as âlegal proceedingsâ could be more operationalised, but overall the question is precise, focused and tightly connected to discussion.

The report follows IB EE conventions: title page, table of contents, numbered headings and logical progression from introduction to conclusion.  Each section is clearly signposted, making navigation straightforward.  All required structural elements are present, satisfying the highest presentation band.

The discussion weighs reliability versus unreliability arguments, integrating ethical and legal implications.  Evaluative comments on publication bias and generalisability show critical stance.  Nonetheless, recommendations for future research remain broad and some conclusions (e.g., eyewitness testimony can be as reliable as DNA) are stated more forcefully than the evidence warrants.  The argument is reasoned and partially critical, aligning with the 7-9 descriptorâs lower range.

Analysis goes beyond description: mechanisms such as cortisolâs hippocampal effects and schema-driven reconstruction are unpacked, and methodological flaws (e.g., student samples, self-report bias) are critiqued.  Yet depth is unevenâstatistical strength (effect sizes, confidence intervals) is rarely traced, and some assertions (confidenceâaccuracy correlation) are accepted without full causal explanation.  The analysis is clear and largely supported, fitting the lower end of the 7-9 band.

annotations.comment: Reference list entries show inconsistent hanging indents; apply uniform indentation for professional formatting.
Concluding critique on research focus is insightful; consider adding specific future research questions to address the identified gap.
Conclusion effectively synthesizes supportive and critical evidence, leading logically to recommendations for legal practice improvements.
Publication bias discussion would benefit from referencing meta-analytic tools (e.g., funnel plot) used to detect skew in results.
Final sentence of the conclusion is incomplete; ensure the last paragraph delivers a succinct and definitive closing statement.
Research question is precisely stated in italics and effectively sets the focus for the investigation.
Word count is provided but enclosed in parentheses; ensure accurate count and placement without brackets to meet formatting requirements.
Table of contents clearly lists all required sections with appropriate page references, enhancing navigation.
Transitions between discussions of reconstructive memory and flashbulb memories are abrupt; add signposting sentences to improve flow.
Integration of the cortisolâhippocampus mechanism from Kim & Diamond (2002) provides a clear biological rationale for memory impairment under stress.
Gabbert et al. (2003) results are summarised without percentages or significance levels; include these statistics to substantiate your analysis.
Reliance on foundational studies from the 1940s may limit temporal validity; integrate recent replications to provide balanced evidence.
Title is clearly stated at the start of the report, providing a concise focus for the investigation.
Layout of the contents table is consistent and aids quick access to each section.
The term âexternal factorsâ is introduced but not operationally defined; specify its components early to clarify the studyâs scope.
Methodology is implied as a literature review but lacks explicit detail on source selection criteria, search strategy, and screening procedures.
Effect sizes and statistical significance from key studies are described qualitatively; including numerical values would deepen analytical rigor.
Critique of Yuille & Cutshall (1986) would benefit from discussion of potential interviewer-induced reporting biases after the four-month delay.
The claim that familiarity improves recall would be strengthened by including recall accuracy statistics broken down by relationship level.
The citation âGREER, 1971â appears in all caps; standard APA uses sentence case for author names, so adjust to âGreer (1971)â.
Terminology such as âweapon focus effectâ and âpost-event informationâ is introduced accurately and applied consistently throughout.
Consider presenting a table comparing internal and external validity of each study to enhance clarity and structure.
Argument on retrospective self-report bias is strong; to deepen evaluation, suggest alternative methods like real-time physiological data collection.
Description of Loftus et al. (1987) omits participant demographics and sample size, which are necessary to assess generalisability.
Literature review lacks a dedicated methodology subsection outlining databases searched, keywords used, and inclusion/exclusion criteria.
Summary of Newcomer et al. (1999) lacks detail on cortisol dosage and measurement protocols; include these to demonstrate command of experimental design.
Cultural bias critique in Yarmey (1993) would be stronger with discussion of specific cultural dimensions (e.g., individualism vs. collectivism).
Limitation of Loftus & Palmerâs (1974) driver experience could be quantified by comparing speed estimation error rates across participant groups.
Application of dual-process model and System 1 thinking to explain memory distortion shows strong conceptual integration.
Insightful critique of reductionism in biological studies underscores the need to consider sociocultural influences on memory.
Sharot et al. (2006) study reference omits threshold criteria for proximity to 9/11 site; specify the distance measure used for clarity.
The link between low-stress conditions and improved recall from Newcomer et al. (1999) is well articulated, showing nuanced understanding.
McGaugh et al. (1996) critique notes low replicability; suggest methodological enhancements, such as multiple case studies, to strengthen applicability.
Loftus & Pickrell (1995) false-memory findings are noted without confidence intervals; adding CI will illustrate the reliability of the 25% rate.
Sampling bias critique is apt; reference exemplar cross-age or cross-cultural studies to illustrate variability across demographics.
URLs in the reference list break mid-line; use DOI links or non-breaking spaces to prevent awkward line breaks.
Ensure consistency in italicising titles: some journal and book titles are formatted inconsistently across entries.
